name: System Design: Architecture Patterns
description:
Explore **software architecture patterns** through visual diagrams.

This quiz uses [Mermaid](https://mermaid.js.org/) diagrams to illustrate system components, data flows, and architectural trade-offs.

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client([Client]) --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> S1[Server 1]
  LB --> S2[Server 2]
  S1 --> DB[(Database)]
  S2 --> DB
```

questions.text:
The following diagram shows a common horizontal scaling pattern. What is the role of the middle component?

```mermaid
graph LR
  C1([Client 1]) --> LB[?]
  C2([Client 2]) --> LB
  LB --> A[App Server A]
  LB --> B[App Server B]
  LB --> C[App Server C]
```

choices.text: Load Balancer — distributes incoming requests across multiple servers
API Gateway — transforms and routes API calls
Message Broker — decouples producers from consumers

questions.explanation:
A **Load Balancer** distributes incoming client requests across a pool of backend servers. This improves availability and throughput — if one server fails, traffic is routed to the remaining healthy ones.

Common algorithms: round-robin, least connections, IP hash.

questions.text:
Which architecture does this diagram represent?

```mermaid
graph TD
  UI[Frontend] --> API[API Gateway]
  API --> US[User Service]
  API --> OS[Order Service]
  API --> PS[Payment Service]
  US --> UDB[(Users DB)]
  OS --> ODB[(Orders DB)]
  PS --> PDB[(Payments DB)]
```
questions.explanation:
This is a **microservices architecture**. Each service (User, Order, Payment) is independent, has its own database, and communicates through an API Gateway.

Key traits:
- Services are independently deployable
- Each service owns its data store
- Failure in one service doesn't crash the whole system

Contrast with a **monolith**, where all logic lives in one deployable unit sharing a single database.

questions.text:
Study this event-driven flow. What is the component labelled **MQ**?

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant Producer
  participant MQ as MQ
  participant ConsumerA
  participant ConsumerB

  Producer->>MQ: publish(OrderPlaced)
  MQ-->>ConsumerA: deliver event
  MQ-->>ConsumerB: deliver event
  ConsumerA->>ConsumerA: send confirmation email
  ConsumerB->>ConsumerB: update inventory
```
questions.explanation:
**MQ** stands for **Message Queue** (or Message Broker, e.g. RabbitMQ, Kafka).

In event-driven architecture, the producer publishes an event and doesn't need to know which consumers will process it. The broker delivers the event to all interested subscribers asynchronously.

Benefits:
- Decouples producers from consumers
- Enables fan-out (one event → many handlers)
- Improves resilience (consumers can process at their own pace)

questions.text:
In the diagram below, which components would you add to reduce database load and improve response times? Select all that apply.

```mermaid
graph LR
  User --> App[Application Server]
  App --> DB[(Primary DB)]
```

choices.text: In-memory cache (e.g. Redis) between App and DB
Read replica database for read-heavy queries
CDN for caching static assets at the edge

questions.explanation:
Three effective strategies:

1. **In-memory cache (Redis/Memcached)** — stores frequent query results in RAM; eliminates repeated DB hits for hot data.
2. **Read replicas** — dedicated DB instances that handle SELECT queries, offloading the primary DB.
3. **CDN** — caches static files (images, JS, CSS) geographically close to users, reducing origin server load.

A second active-active primary without careful coordination leads to write conflicts and is not a straightforward solution.

questions.text: What does **stateless** mean in the context of web servers?
questions.explanation:
A **stateless** server does not store any client session data between requests. Each request contains all the information needed to process it (e.g. via JWT tokens or session IDs looked up in a shared store).

```mermaid
graph LR
  C[Client] -->|request + token| S1[Server 1]
  C -->|next request + token| S2[Server 2]
  S1 --- Cache[(Shared Cache)]
  S2 --- Cache
```

Stateless servers are easy to scale horizontally — any server can handle any request because none of them hold local session state.
